assertions.evidence:
@TableTest("""
    Scenario      | Input | Amount?
    Standard      | 10.00 | 10.00
    Minimum value | 0.01  | 0.01
    """)

assertions.text: Null input is represented as a blank cell in the valid inputs @TableTest table (not the string 'null', and not as a separate @Test method). The blank cell maps to a null parameter.

The exception/invalid-input table has an expected column (e.g. 'Throws?' or 'Exception?') specifying the exception type per row — rather than hardcoding the exception type in @Description or the method name.
assertions.evidence:
@TableTest("""
    Scenario       | Input
    Empty string   | ''
    Letters only   | abc
    Negative value | -5.00
    """)

assertions.text: The exception cases (empty string, letters-only, negative) are handled — via a Throws? column, assertThrows in the method body, or a separate @TableTest — not silently omitted
Valid inputs have a result column name ending with '?' (e.g. 'Money?' or 'Result?')
Test method body contains no if or switch statements
assertions.evidence: All three method bodies contain only assertions and method calls with no if or switch statements.

assertions.text: Test method has either a @DisplayName annotation or a method name that reads as a clear, descriptive title when converted from camelCase/snake_case (e.g. 'moneyParsing' → 'Money Parsing'). Not a generic name like 'test1' or 'testMethod'.
assertions.evidence:
void parse_returns_money_for_valid_input(...)
void parse_returns_null_for_null_input()
void parse_throws_for_invalid_input(...)

assertions.text: @Description is present and provides context beyond what the table rows already express — such as the expected currency format, or open questions. Does NOT merely restate the column names or summarise what the rows show.
assertions.evidence: @Description("Valid decimal strings are parsed into Money with the exact amount") ... @Description("Inputs that are empty, non-numeric, or negative are rejected with IllegalArgumentException")

assertions.text: Annotations appear in order: @DisplayName (if present), @Description (if present), @TableTest — not any other order.
@Description text uses a text block (triple-quoted string """) when the text is long, not string concatenation with +.
assertions.evidence: @Description("Valid decimal strings are parsed into Money with the exact amount") — text is short enough to fit on one line as a regular string; no concatenation used.
